messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Present the work plan with work packages, tasks, deliverables, milestones, and Gantt logic.
Write the Work plan and work packages section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3.1 Work plan  
Our AI-CUBE project will be implemented through 3 key project phases:  
  
1. Phase 1: Set-Up - Stakeholder engagement strategy, MLA methodology and initial technology assessment  
2. Phase 2: Gap analysis - AI-CUBE business cases and gap analysis vs. current STATUS  
3. Phase 3: Roadmapping - translate gap analysis into skills, data and RD&I requirements per SPIRE process industry sector  
4. Phase 4: Take-up - Disseminate and promote the AI-CUBE roadmaps towards the wider process industries stakeholder communities  
The activities of our project will be structured in 6 work packages (WPs) as shown in fig. 16.  
  
Figure 16. PERT Chart  
  
In the set-up phase 1 of our project, WP1 will start with an extensive literature and stakeholder analysis, to establish the current technological and industrial landscape regarding AI and BD technologies and process industry sectors in Europe, setting the basis of the following WPs work in defining mapping tools and the roadmap for AI and BD. In parallel, in WP2 a stakeholder engagement strategy will be set-up based on the MAMCA methodology, to work in a continuous way with the stakeholders and to design a framework to assess and categorize current Maturity Levels of AI and BD technologies’ applications and deployment. Phase 2 of our project will be targeted at carrying out a gap-analysis. First, WP3 will assess the maturity level of the previously identified technologies, while WP4 will define AI and BD driven Business Cases and Business Models for the process industries of the future. In WP4 a mapping of such visionary Business Cases and Models against the current STATUS (WP3) will be carried out to establish gaps and development needs per SPIRE process industry sector and per macro-application process areas (as defined in the CUBE). In the roadmapping phase 3, results from the previous phases will be translated into development needs and recommendations in terms of skills, data and RD&I requirements. As such WP4 will define a roadmap on AI and BD application in process industries for all the SPIRE industrial sectors. WP5 and WP6 will be horizontal to the whole project, focusing on dissemination and project coordination respectively, and culminating in the last Take-up phase 4, where the results of our project (the CUBEs, MLA framework, learnings and roadmaps) will be promoted towards the wider process industries communities (starting with > 100 industrial parks already identified in the previous INSPIRE project) and working in close collaboration with A.SPIRE.  
Based on previous experience with SPIRE and FoF CSA’s, our AI-CUBE consortium aims for an early involvement of A.SPIRE in the project, already discussed with the A.SPIRE office. This will be leveraged to steer our project towards optimal and usable results, that can be taken-up by the eight (plus possibly pulp and paper, glass, etc.) process industries, as they come available in the course of our project.  
  
Figure 17. AI-CUBE Gantt chart

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Present the work plan with work packages, tasks, deliverables, milestones, and Gantt logic.
Write the Work plan and work packages section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3.1 Work plan — Work packages, deliverables  
In line with the technological objectives that we have to overcome in this 48-month project, the work to be accomplished has been structured with the following work packages (WP) (Figure 3.1), and each WP has been sub-divided into a number of tasks, which are listed in detail in Figure 3.2 (Gantt chart). Each WP will be led and managed by a project partner who is an expert in the relevant field. The WP structure aims to set the basis for new bio-based value chains with a focus on creating new cross-sector interconnections in bio-based economy clusters. The objectives will address issues raised by the NMBP-04-2020 topic and will be implemented in a reasonable and cost-effective manner. WP objectives are built on integrated production methods, delivery of added value polymers, cost-effective accurate characterizations, and variability of product evaluation through LCA approach. We will generate knowledge, develop novel bioprocessing technologies, and industrial concepts transferrable to upscale testing. All 35 partners will collaborate in order to successfully deliver and achieve the BIOMAC objectives.  

Figure 3.1: Work package structure of BIOMAC OITB.

3.2.5  Risk and mitigation measures  
We have split our project risks into three main categories: 1) Technology & implementation risks, 2) Management Risks, and 3) Ecosystem Sustainability risks which cover also transversal risks (related to dissemination and exploitation). Given the dynamic nature of any project of this size and complexity, we have set a well-defined risk management process and specific procedures to reassure early identification, adequate handling, and proper mitigation.  

Table 3.3: Critical risks for implementation (P: Probability, I: Impact)

The relevant task dedicated to LCA includes a cost-benefit analysis of the proposed technologies, which in turn determine the “boundary conditions” for their successful introduction to the market. Strategies will be economically compared with current processes information retrieved by the end users.

Promotion of our project and benefits of entering the ecosystem are essential to achieve the engagement of all stakeholders along different sectors. A proper training to the general public about the advantages will be conducted. We have already collected an appropriate amount of LoI; these companies will be requested additionally to send their applications for the open calls.

conditions P: l. I: m 8  
This risk has been mitigated by establishing IPR rules as is documented in Section 2.2. In addition, before project start, all of us sign the Consortium Agreement. IRP clauses are clear and understood by every member; even if a dispute occurs, a loyal firm will be sought to handle.

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Present the work plan with work packages, tasks, deliverables, milestones, and Gantt logic.
Write the Work plan and work packages section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3.1 Work plan — Work packages, deliverables and milestones  
3.1.1 Overall structure and Pert Chart  
A competitive work plan has been devised in order to deliver on the objectives of our CEM-WAVE project to ensure that we fulfill our goals and vision to catalyse the activities to produce novel materials capable of resisting fluctuation and extreme future conditions and to provide the requirements of our CEM-WAVE use case. KPIs have been used as the starting point for planning the work that needs to be implemented within our project. All the actions that are required to meet the objectives of our Research and Innovation Action have been collectively analysed by our interdisciplinary consortium. We agreed on how best to distribute the workload among the various experts by making best use of their relevant fields of knowledge, capacity, infrastructure, and resources. This bottom-up planning process has greatly facilitated our task of arriving at a clear, comprehensive, and realistic work plan (see Gantt Chart and Pert Diagram below), whereby all partners have ownership of our work plan and its approach. Of a total of 10 WPs aimed at accomplishing the broad scope of this SPIRE topic as previously justified, our R&D work is split into 6 WPs, connected with the one in which a use case will be analyzed, each with clear tasks that will enable our project’s objectives to be fulfilled through a coherent structure that will also facilitate project management (WP10), sustainability study (WP8), and the further dissemination and exploitation of the results (WP9).  

Stakeholder CEM-WAVE Benefits and Communication strategy  
- Steelmaking industries  
  - Production and use of innovative, sustainable equipment/products for the steelmaking sector: contacted through online forums, mailing, events and congresses, advertising in sector-related magazines.  
  - Knowledge of the environmental impact of different materials.  
  - Risk identification on time will have a positive impact on the existing businesses.  
- CMCs industry, Composite Manufacturers, Material Matrix Manufacturers, Ceramic matrix composite distributors and suppliers  
  - Articles in specialized journals and magazines.  
  - Sectorial conferences.  
  - Posters/Oral Presentations.  
- CMC converters  
  - Articles in specialized journals and magazines.  
  - Sectorial conferences.  
  - Posters/Oral Presentations.  
- Standardisation community  
  - Project information and dissemination of project results.  
- Academia  
  - Articles in specialized journals and magazines.  
  - Sectorial conferences.  
  - Posters/Oral Presentations.  
- General public  
  - Information about CEM-WAVE and its achievements.  
  - Public website, datasheets creation, marketing campaigns  

Figure 3.1 – CEM-WAVE Pert Chart.  

The first research phase (WP1 and WP2) deals with the preparation of the fiber coating and improves our knowledge of the solid-state MW technology. The heart of CEM-WAVE is characterized by WP3, in which oxide and non-oxide CMCs will be produced with the implemented MW-CVI technique. All around there are WPs of support about the WP3 activity: AI-assisted modeling (WP5), Characterization also in relevant environments (WP4), and joining R&D on CMCs (WP6). Concurrently, the CMCs we gradually design, produce, and join will be tested by our CEM-WAVE use case work package (WP7). As described before, our work plan is encased in a robust environmental and thermoeconomical analysis (WP8), in a fundamental project exploitation, dissemination, and communication plan (WP9), and in a well-established and efficient project management structure and procedures (WP10). Our work plan is linked to the objectives set out in Section 1.1, and we are confident that this work plan is robust to ensure that our project’s S&T and innovation goals are fulfilled.  

3.1.2 Gantt chart  
3.1.3 Detailed work packages description

Objectives  
- Development of oxide and non-oxide preforms to be processed in WP3 by microwave CVI assisted densification. Moreover, the preform will be used for joining experiments in WP6  
- Different fibre preform architectures will be required. The preform thickness will be between 3 mm to 9 mm  
- Different fibre lay-up architectures and fibre orientations will be investigated to achieve an appropriate porosity  
- For the primary stabilization of the fibre preforms, binder systems will be developed  
- Total porosity and the pore size distribution of the preforms will need to be adjusted  

Description of work  
Firstly, we will define the technical and specific requirements for applications of both oxide and non-oxide CMCs in more detail by AM. This includes especially the sample geometries and maximum service temperature. At the beginning of the process chain for the CMC production, both oxide and non-oxide ceramic fibres need to be coated to adjust the interfacial domain between fibre and matrix for the production of Weak Interface Composites, WICs (Task 1.1 & Task 1.2), e.g., non-oxide and oxide CMCs with low matrix porosity. Note that uncoated preforms will be required for the Weak Matrix Composites, WMCs, e.g., for the oxide composites with high matrix porosity. At FHF, we will coat ceramic fibres continuously in a roll-to-roll process by a wet-chemical route. The technology can provide coated fibres in sufficient quantity and in a reasonable time. The coated fibres will be delivered to Task 1.3, where we will develop preforms with selected geometries by techniques such as filament winding or prepreg technology based on 2D fabrics. Three manufacturing loops will be needed to achieve the most suitable preform quality. Commercially available SiC fibres like 2nd or 3rd generation SiC fibres (e.g., UBE-ZMI, UBE-Tyranno SA, NGS-Hi Nicalon, NGS Hi-Nicalon type S) as well as oxide fibres (e.g., 3M-Nextel 610 & 720) will be necessary to allow manufacturing and application temperatures up to 1200°C. The preforms can either be infiltrated first by MW-CVI or be joined first and then infiltrated.  

Task 1.1 Coating set-up of non-oxide fibres for CMC's production (M1-10)  
Non-oxide ceramic fibres will be coated in a continuous roll-to-roll process at FHF. We will optimize the coating parameters to achieve homogeneous coatings with a high degree of coverage and minimized fibre bridging. The latter will be of great importance to have flexible tows after application of the coatings and, in turn, flexible tows are required in Task 1.4 to develop a stable process for the preparation of the preforms by filament winding techniques. An interface system based on BN and PyC with additional layers of Si

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Present the work plan with work packages, tasks, deliverables, milestones, and Gantt logic.
Write the Work plan and work packages section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3.1 Work plan and resources Brief presentation of the overall structure of our work plan Our project workplan has been designed to allow us, over the course of 4 years, and in an application-oriented approach to develop, test, optimize, scale-up and validate (from TLR3 to TRL6), the following innovative and scalable technologies that are -by- -by- - pharmaceutical manufacture (and that are also less environmentally harmful during use and disposal): HME (hot melt extrusion); Discontinuous Synthesis (batch reaction) using Isobutyl and acetone; Rapid microfluidic mixing combined with Diafiltration; Modular flow reactors continuous systems; Genome engineering for strain optimization, bioprocessing intensification and microjet reactor technology (MJR) for downstreaming processes; Membrane purification. WP1 (Green innovation in pharmaceutical manufacturing) focuses on the R&D phase to bring the technologies from TRL3-4, and WP3 (Industry cocreation lab: Pilots) will further scale up to TRL5-6. WP2 (Digitalisation for competitive & scalable methods of production) will develop digital technologies (digital work flows for chemical data, PAT and Digital Twins) to facilitate the process develop and understanding. In parallel, WP4 (Environmental assessment & ecotoxicity studies) will, in addition to performing environmental risk and life cycle analysis within our project case studies, will also bridge the current knowledge gaps in relation to the environmental concentration and resulting levels of risk of pharmaceuticals already in use, and in particular, building knowledge of the impact of molecules on the environment through the eco-toxicity studies for improved management of environmental risk and for designing of new molecules. From a multi-stakeholder and a social innovation perspective, WP5 (Communication, Dissemination & Change labs), will ensure we engage decision-makers from the wider pharmaceutical industry, public authorities, healthcare practitioners as well as citizens themselves towards an integrated movement behind catalysing not only the adoption of green pharmaceutical technologies, but also promoting an enabling regulatory environment, as well as paving the way forward for changing social norms for safe consumption and disposal of pharmaceuticals (and also considering gendered innovation aspects). Finally, WP6 (Project Management and Pathway to impact) will ensure we encase our project in a robust and comprehensive project management framework, that in addition to project monitoring, tracking and risk management, also encompasses data management, management of Open Science, Standardization activities, as well as preparation for the post-project exploitation phase towards further scale up industrial deployment of the results, and ensuring wide adoption across the EU of the ETERNAL outputs for environmental and competitiveness impacts across the European pharmaceutical industry. Graphical presentation of the components showing how they interrelate (Pert chart or similar) ETERNAL (HORIZON-HLTH-2021-IND-07-01) Page 32 of 45 Table 3.1a: List of work packages Table 3.1b: Work package description Objectives: Our goal is to perform R&D work TRL3-TRL4 of our project case studies in preparation for the piloting of the technological innovations in industry in WP3 (there will also be an inter-relationship with WP2, whereby the aim will be to develop PAT and digital twins to understand the processes and facilitate their upscaling); To apply continuous methods to reduce energy consumption and solvents, both in the synthesis of APIs and medicines production; To improve or develop new purification routes for solvents recovery (recycling). To develop greener nanoparticle manufacturing methods that provide nano formulations of the same quality as classic methods. WP1 is linked to Task 2..6 Compliance by design. Description of work (where appropriate, broken down into tasks), lead partner and role of participants Task 1.1.1. Preliminary evaluation & status quo on HME. We will perform a review of the state of the art with the intention to apply this technology to more pharmaceutical products owned by Angelini (to go beyond the case study developed). Chemical-physical properties of the compounds will be investigated to verify suitability for HME technology. Current manufacturing processes medicines that contain Ibuprofen and Trazodone will be analysed in depth for changing to continuous manufacturing (HME). In addition to the study on the active ingredient, we will carry out research activities on polymers, excipients and plasticizers potentially usable in extrusion processes. Task 1.1.2 Preformulating trials and formulation trials: We will perform first laboratory tests on Ibuprofen and Trazodone: Compatibility studies (API and excipients); Thermal characterization of API: polymer: plasticizer mixtures; Extrusion trials of different preliminary mixture (DoE based on screw configuration, screw speed, throughput and temperature profile); Chemical-physical analyses on preliminary extrudates; Definition of the composition; Stress tests; Trials of tablet compression or filling in capsules; 12 month prestability study. Task 1.1.3. Analytical development & stability studies: We will develop new pharmaceutical processes regarding new formulations, dedicated analytical support (both physical and chemical) focused on trials and analysis is needed. Indeed, analytical data on the quality and properties of the product are necessary to gain the detailed process knowledge. Several instruments such as HPLC, Dissolution apparatus, X-ray diffractometer, will be used for this study. Main analytical activities include Analytical development; In parallel analytical methods will be developed, depending on product specifications. The analytical procedures will provide an acceptable accuracy & precision over the specified range; Raw materials, half-processed and finished products will be analysed and selected prototypes will also be investigated using a stress test protocol; Stability-At the end of the previous studies, 12 months prestability of drug product will be performed at ICH conditions. This task will focus on the synthesis of trazodone (antidepressant API) T1.2.1. Recycling Solvents: the synthesis of trazodone implies the use of 2k tons of solvents per year. The solvents used in the process are acetone and isobutyl alcohol. To improve the current system for the distillation and to capture impurities and reagents (we will follow the ICH quality guidelines), we will carry out the following steps: Preliminary in silico assessments and simulations of the distillation; Laboratory trials (using equipment and pilot/real mixtures): considering previous simulations, AIMPLAS will implement at laboratory scale the better combination of filters to separate both solvents and to capture the impurities (including Chitosan for capturing impurities). Together with BOKU, we will carry out the degradation of those substances using enzymatic steps. The quality of purified solvents will be evaluated by Angelini by gas chromatography (GC) optimizing the filtering sequence if it is needed. T1.2.2. Greener alternatives: We will carry out the synthesis of trazodone at laboratory scale using more sustainable alternatives. Biobased solvents will be studied (e.g. ethyl lactate, to replace acetone, 2-methyltetrahydrofuran (2-MeTHF) to replace isobutyl). Biobased alternatives, e.g. acetone could be supplied by Caldic -produced through fermentation of sugars from renewable resources, and free of all carcinogenic aromatic impurities (e.g. benzene) that are commonly found in traditionally sourced materials. Trials will be done at AIMPLAS facilities & we will oversee quality evaluation of obtained trazodone (HPLC analysis). Task 1.2. Case Study II (Angelini Pharma): Better use of solvents (recycled and Greener alternatives) (Leader: Angelini Pharma Participants: AIMPLAS, BOKU). Task 1.1 Case Study I-(Angelini Pharma): Continuous manufacturing method-Reduction in energy consumption (Leader: Angelini Pharma Participants: AIMPLAS, EE). ETERNAL (HORIZON-HLTH-2021-IND-07-01) Page 33 of 45 Task 1.3 Case Study III (RJ): Better use of solvents (reduction or elimination). (Leader: Reig Jofre Participants: AIMPLAS, BOKU, UAVR). RJF will oversee developing greener methods to produce Liposomes at laboratory scale. Using liposomes, the bioavailability of API is achieved. This results in a reduction of the required dose to achieve the same pharmacological effect. Reducing the dose and increasing bioavailability increases drug absorption and reduces excretion from the gastrointestinal tract. On a large scale this leads to a reduction of metabolites excreted into the environment. One of the objectives of this task is to increase the encapsulation efficiency of the API, selected, valproic acid and other hydrophilic drugs. The increase of encapsulation efficiency could be achieved by the proper selection of formulation excipients, optimization of process parameters and the selection of more advanced manufacturing methods. T1.3.1. Microfluidic manufacturing methods: We will focus on this technology as it allows continuous and scale-up-reproducible production of liposomes, using ethanol as organic solvent. Also, the encapsulation efficacy of this production technique is usually higher, around 50-60% for hydrophilic compounds. RJF will test the micro mixing technologies from MyBiotech and will compare them with other microfluidics technologies available on the market. The integration of an on-line particle size analyser at the exit of the microfluidic device and before the entry in the diafiltration device will improve the continuous process production (developed in T2.5); T1.3.2. Liposome production with greener solvents: we propose to test the use of a better toxicological and safety profile (ethyl lactate, ethyl pyruvate, DMC, etc.) during liposome production, to check if they are able to keep the liposome desired

(1) Define, analyse, and consolidate user requirements (2) Compile technological and system integration specifications (3) Construct a plan for the involvement of local communities and regional stakeholders Description of Work – WP Leader: IVE T1.1 Definition of end user requirements - (IVE), M01-M06 This task will consolidate end user related information and deliver a comprehensive report refining the goals of the demonstrations. The demonstrators (AUMSA, MWNB, DVVV) will provide feedback with regards to (a) existing technical and non-technical issues with their properties, and (b) their expectations from INPERSO. The conclusions will be used to facilitate the communication between us, provide common goals and establish solid foundations for the dissemination, exploitation and communication activities. Specifically, the following aspects will be consolidated: • User requirements (UPV): Technical (i.e., building design specifications), budgetary (how much is intended to be invested for the new construction or retrofit) and functional (energy performance and operation, additional functions such as storage, EV charging etc). • Consolidation of available building designs and BIM assets (DMO) from the demonstration sites. A systematic review and collection of available plans and information will be carried out to lay the groundwork for the planned interventions. • Stakeholder mapping relevant to each neighbourhood (TAU) Partners involved: Demonstrators will provide requirements. Output: Consolidated end-user requirements included in D1.1. T1.2 Consolidation of technological and system specifications - (UPV), M01-M06 Specifications relevant to the technologies, processes and tools employed will be consolidated to ensure interoperability is not compromised and that synergies are maximized. Selection of sensor and IoT devices will be conducted based on data requirements including parametric scenarios based on both real-time data (or almost real-time) values of energy (thermal – electricity consumption), insolation, occupancy, shading, window positions and other parameters relevant to our work plan. We will provide input with regards to requirements (the level of optimization and cost savings we would consider satisfactory) and preferences (intrusiveness of additional electronics, cost etc). The finalized selection will be accompanied by a refined and consolidated set of KPIs agreed by us. Partners involved: All partners will be involved. Output: Consolidated technical specifications included in D1.1. T1.3 Identification and plan for the involvement of local stakeholders - (IVE), M01-M06 The human dimension will be placed in the core of each stage of our project and the resulting service. To design a solution that is useful to humans, it is needed we are engaged in a process known as human-centred design. Human-centred design is not a perfectly linear process, and each project invariably has its own contours and character. The process is best described as a “converge-diverge” cycle, where we go big in our thinking, dial down into specifics and practicality, look back at the big picture, and iterate. Figure 18 Source: The Field Guide to Human-Centered Design This process will be expanded in our project through a stakeholder-centred approach as an iterative process where interaction with the different stakeholders (users, technology/service providers) will be carried out to refine the final output to: Analyse and cluster the demand-side and specify the development drivers for the different technologies/services from a user-centred approach. The stakeholder-centred approach will be used to know the drivers for the different technologies from the different stakeholders’ point of view to help technology providers to improve their final products. 3.1 Work plan — Work packages, deliverables

Brief presentation of the overall structure of our work plan: The brief overview of our work plan and interconnection between different WPs is shown in the PERT figure below. We start with WP1 establishing requirements of high yield manufacturing parameters, inspection needs and material challenges to achieve reliability of components and longer lifetimes promoting sustainability thus less waste and material to be recycled thus contributing to circular economy. Further, the sensors and automation of R2R and S2S lines will also be analysed in WP1. In WP2, innovative deposition, packaging and patterning techniques and processes will be optimised for industrial scaled up implementation. WP3 will deal with inspection system, techniques and software development. WP4 will incorporate automation, sensor system, hardware and intelligent decision management software. WP5 will develop recycling and re-use strategies and technologies to promote sustainability. WP6 will include testing and trial at pilot scale as well as integration and validation activities in production lines of end user partners. WP7 will include dissemination and exploitation activities and WP8 will outline project management, coordination guidelines and implementation.

Organisation Description: We, VTT Technical Research Centre of Finland Ltd, are a state owned and controlled non-profit limited liability company established by law and operating under the ownership steering of the Finnish Ministry of Employment and the Economy. We are an RTO whose activities are focused on three areas: Knowledge intensive products and services, Smart industry and energy systems, and Solutions for natural resources and environment. We are impact-driven and take advantage of our wide multi-technological knowledge base to strengthen Finnish and European industrial competitiveness. We can combine different technologies, produce information, upgrade technology knowledge, and create business intelligence and value added for our stakeholders. We have a staff of 2128, net turnover in 2016 was 162,6M€ and other operational incomes were 86,4M€. Over the years, we have gained vast experience from participation and coordination of numerous European projects including R&D Framework Programme projects and other thematic frameworks and programmes. We are ranked among the leading European RTOs.

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Present the work plan with work packages, tasks, deliverables, milestones, and Gantt logic.
Write the Work plan and work packages section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3.1 Work plan — Work packages, deliverables  
3.1.1. Overall structure of the work plan  
Our PHOTONFOOD project will be implemented by an interdisciplinary consortium of academic, authority, and industry partners covering 14 organizations from eight European countries. Our project duration will be 48 months, and the breakdown of the planned work into work packages (WPs) is based on accomplishing related work rather than on single tasks that occur at the same time. Therefore, several WPs will be executed in parallel throughout our project. Our project consists of eight work packages (WPs) (see Fig. 15), where the first WP1 is related to the evaluation of the technology acceptance and techno-economical evaluation, four WPs (WP2 – WP5) focused on the photonic technology developments and developing advanced data analysis tools and software applications, WP6 will perform validation and demonstration of PHOTONFOOD technology, and WP7 and WP8 are related to dissemination, communication, exploitation, and management activities, respectively (Fig. 15). The general overview of WPs is provided below; the list of the WPs and a detailed description of each WP are provided in Table 3.1a and 3.1b, respectively.  
Assisting WPs:  
WP1 “Technology acceptance and techno-economical evaluation” will conduct a comprehensive study on evaluating user expectations, experience, and technology acceptance of photonic-based technologies for identifying microbiological and chemical contaminations. WP1 will ensure that PHOTONFOOD technology is developed in accordance with user requirements.  
WP7 “Communication, dissemination, and exploitation” will perform adequate dissemination and exploitation of PHOTONFOOD technology and will be responsible for communication with the relevant audience.  
WP8 “Coordination and Management” will be responsible for project coordination and management for the successful implementation of all project work activities.  
Technological WPs:  
Four technological WPs will be responsible for the successful development of PHOTONFOOD technology.  
WP2 “Paper Microfluidics” focuses on the development of extraction/separation/up-concentration technology for the separation of analyte markers of microbial and chemical contamination. WP2 will be strongly connected to photon-based device developing WPs, WP3 and WP4.  
WP3 “Novel MIR light sources” will focus on the development of cascaded gain materials forming the basis for advanced light-emitting diode (IC-LEDs) and monolithically tunable laser light sources.  
WP4 “Modular sensor platform” will implement the results of WP3 and will perform the development of portable and field-deployable (prototype) MI-FI and HI-FI analyzers.  
WP5 “Data analysis and software development” will focus on developing and utilizing advanced data analysis tools for the spectral-based detection and identification of microbiological and chemical contamination in different food matrices. In addition, WP5 will be focused on developing software applications.  
Figure 15: Overall structure of the workplan  
PHOTONFOOD  
48 Demonstration WP:  
WP6 “Demonstration and validation” will focus on demonstrating and validating the developed PHOTONFOOD technology and a vision for farm-to-fork sensing in three case studies. In each case study, monitoring data acquired by portable photonics-based devices will enable quick on-site risk assessment by us, the end-users, at different entry points of the food chains. The GANTT chart in Fig. 16 provides an overview of all work package tasks and includes the milestones, validation phases, and meetings (Table 3.2a).

| Objective: | To conduct comprehensive research on user expectations, user experience, and technology acceptance for a portable, easy-to-use, broadly adaptable photonics device. The specific objectives are: |  
• Identify relevant stakeholders, explore user requirements and expectations towards the innovative multi-purpose photonic sensor solution.  
• Organize the collection of user experience during the validation and demonstration process in WP6.  
• Conduct comparative cost assessment for the MI-FI and HI-FI devices and reference methods considering realistic conditions.  
• Assess user experience and technology acceptance among target users and stakeholders.  
Task 1.1 Stakeholder expectations and user requirements survey (M1-M42) (lead partner: NMBU; participants: BOKU, ROMER, NEBIH, Seeberger, BAMA, WU). Since the exact apparatus of the portable PHOTONFOOD platform cannot be fully defined before the beginning of our project, we will follow an agile approach to user requirements. A kick-off workshop involving (a) the PHOTONFOOD WP leaders and (b) representatives of the prospective user organizations involved in our project (ROMER, NEBIH, Seeberger) will be organized at the beginning of our project (M1) to define a questionnaire for a stakeholder survey. The stakeholder survey will consist of an online questionnaire and a structured follow-up telephone interview. It will be conducted in Austria, Germany, Hungary, the Netherlands, and Norway, covering 15 organizations per country from different levels of the food chain (3 from agriculture, 3 processors, 3 retailers, 4 labs, and 2 border inspection points). At the project kick-off meeting, we, the PHOTONFOOD task leaders, will develop an initial architecture envisioning. The process will then be iterated throughout the lifetime of our project on an annual basis. The component-specific requirements will be continuously updated within the tasks responsible for component development (WP2 to WP5). The system-level and platform-level requirements will be updated at workshops held during the annual project meetings in M8, M18, M25, and M35. Based on the stakeholder and user requirements, deliverable D1.1: Report on stakeholder and user requirements (M24) will be compiled.  
Task 1.2 Collection and analysis of technical and user experience during validation and demonstration (M24-M34) (lead partner: NEBIH; participants: BOKU, NMBU, ROMER, Seeberger, BAMA). During the demonstration phase (when WP6 is active), system-level and platform-level requirements and actual experience will be collected on a continuous basis in close collaboration between us, the members of the WP6 team, and the users and stakeholders involved in the testing and validation. The parameters measured in WP1 during the validation phase of the innovative photonic device (and in parallel with the reference methods) will include: time requirements, cost requirements, level of skill needed for operation, ergonomics, and user experience. User experience data will be gathered from the three main user groups involved in the demonstration activities (industry, retail, and food inspection services), using qualitative (observations and interviews) and quantitative methods (user surveys). A standard usability framework model will be adopted (UTAUT: Unified theory of acceptance and use of technology) which allows a highly structured analysis of such mixed-methods data. The experiences will be analyzed in a systematic way and presented to the WPs engaged in technology development (WP2 to WP5).

Therefore, several WPs will be executed in parallel throughout our project. Our project consists of eight work packages (WPs) (see Fig. 15), where the first WP1 is related to the evaluation of the technology acceptance and techno-economical evaluation, four WPs (WP2 – WP5) focused on the photonic technology developments and developing advanced data analysis tools and software applications, WP6 will perform validation and demonstration of PHOTONFOOD technology, and WP7 and WP8 are related to dissemination, communication, exploitation, and management activities, respectively (Fig. 15). The general overview of WPs is provided below; the list of the WPs and a detailed description of each WP are provided in Table 3.1a and 3.1b, respectively.  
Assisting WPs:  
WP1 “Technology acceptance and techno-economical evaluation” will conduct a comprehensive study on evaluating user expectations, experience, and technology acceptance of photonic-based technologies for identifying microbiological and chemical contaminations. WP1 will ensure that PHOTONFOOD technology is developed in accordance with user requirements. WP7 “Communication, dissemination, and exploitation” will perform adequate dissemination and exploitation of PHOTONFOOD technology and will be responsible for communication with the relevant audience. WP8 “Coordination and Management” will be responsible for project coordination and management for the successful implementation of all project work activities.  
Technological WPs:  
Four technological WPs will be responsible for the successful development of PHOTONFOOD technology. WP2 “Paper Microfluidics” focuses on the development of extraction/separation/up-concentration technology for the separation of analyte markers of microbial and chemical contamination. WP2 will be strongly connected to photon-based device developing WPs, WP3 and WP4. WP3 “Novel MIR light sources” will focus on the development of cascaded gain materials forming the basis for advanced light-emitting diode (IC-LEDs) and monolithically tunable laser light sources. WP4 “Modular sensor platform” will implement the results of WP3 and will perform the development of portable and field-deployable (prototype) MI-FI and HI-FI analyzers. WP5 “Data analysis and software development” will focus on developing and utilizing advanced data analysis tools for the spectral-based detection and identification of microbiological and chemical contamination in different food matrices. In addition, WP5 will be focused on developing software applications.

PHOTONFOOD  
48  
Demonstration WP:  
WP6 “Demonstration and validation” will focus on demonstrating and validating the developed PHOTONFOOD technology and a vision for farm-to-fork sensing in three case studies. In each case study, monitoring data acquired by portable photonics-based devices will enable quick on-site risk assessment by us, the end-users, at different entry points of the food chains. The GANTT chart in Fig. 16 provides an overview of all work package tasks and includes the milestones, validation phases, and meetings (Table 3.2a).

| Objective: | To conduct comprehensive research on user expectations, user experience, and technology acceptance for a portable, easy-to-use, broadly adaptable photonics device. The specific objectives are: |  
• Identify relevant stakeholders, explore user requirements and expectations towards the innovative multi-purpose photonic sensor solution.  
• Organize the collection of user experience during the validation and demonstration process in WP6.  
• Conduct comparative cost assessment for the MI-FI and HI-FI devices and reference methods considering realistic conditions.  
• Assess user experience and technology acceptance among target users and stakeholders.  
Task 1.1 Stakeholder expectations and user requirements survey (M1-M42) (lead partner: NMBU; participants: BOKU, ROMER, NEBIH, Seeberger, BAMA, WU). Since the exact apparatus of the portable PHOTONFOOD platform cannot be fully defined before the beginning of our project, we will follow an agile approach to user requirements. A kick-off workshop involving (a) the PHOTONFOOD WP leaders and (b) representatives of the prospective user organizations involved in our project (ROMER, NEBIH, Seeberger) will be organized at the beginning of our project (M1) to define a questionnaire for a stakeholder survey. The stakeholder survey will consist of an online questionnaire and a structured follow-up telephone interview. It will be conducted in Austria, Germany, Hungary, the Netherlands, and Norway, covering 15 organizations per country from different levels of the food chain (3 from agriculture, 3 processors, 3 retailers, 4 labs, and 2 border inspection points). At the project kick-off meeting, we, the PHOTONFOOD task leaders, will develop an initial architecture envisioning. The process will then be iterated throughout the lifetime of our project on an annual basis. The component-specific requirements will be continuously updated within the tasks responsible for component development (WP2 to WP5). The system-level and platform-level requirements will be updated at workshops held during the annual project meetings in M8, M18, M25, and M35. Based on the stakeholder and user requirements, deliverable D1.1: Report on stakeholder and user requirements (M24) will be compiled.  
Task 1.2 Collection and analysis of technical and user experience during validation and demonstration (M24-M34) (lead partner: NEBIH; participants: BOKU, NMBU, ROMER, Seeberger, BAMA). During the demonstration phase (when WP6 is active), system-level and platform-level requirements and actual experience will be collected on a continuous basis in close collaboration between us, the members of the WP6 team, and the users and stakeholders involved in the testing and validation. The parameters measured in WP1 during the validation phase of the innovative photonic device (and in parallel with the reference methods) will include: time requirements, cost requirements, level of skill needed for operation, ergonomics, and user experience. User experience data will be gathered from the three main user groups involved in the demonstration activities (industry, retail, and food inspection services), using qualitative (observations and interviews) and quantitative methods (user surveys). A standard usability framework model will be adopted (UTAUT: Unified theory of acceptance and use of technology) which allows a highly structured analysis of such mixed-methods data. The experiences will be analyzed in a systematic way and presented to the WPs engaged in technology development (WP2 to WP5).
